sentences.text: And there was-- there was-- there were Saturdays in winter when my mother usually took me to town.
Saturday afternoon.
There was a very nice pastry shop with little tables, and it was called Bergers, B-- capital B-E-R-G-E-R-S.
And they had the most wonderful hot chocolate with whipped cream and cakes, and pastries.
And my mother met her cousins there, my aunt Irene and my aunt Olga.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was very nice.
That was very nice, usually every Saturday afternoon in winter.
And Sundays were boring, except when my cousin-- should I say the Czech name?
Sure.
Jirka, J-I-R-K-A.
It's-- it's [?
Jiri. ?] He was four or five years older than I.
Except that when he came to visit.
He was the son of my mother's sister.
And when-- when we met him with my aunt Elsa, so that was fine.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were very good friends, even though there was an age difference.
He was-- he was very nice too me.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that was-- that was the extent of my-- [INAUDIBLE] during-- during winter, my mother had a subscription to the Philharmonic.
And sometimes she bought another ticket when-- when she thought the concert was suitable, and she would take me.

And Saturday afternoons sometimes, also in winter, we had school performances in either the national theater or one of the playhouses, and we went with whoever wanted to go from the class, with two teachers.
And that was also great.
We loved it, always.
So I was walked to the theater, and I was picked up at the theater.
I went alone by street car, I think the war broke out, or I was-- when I-- no, that's not true.
I'm exaggerating.
When I was nine and a half and I went to the gymnasium, so the first [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] time I was allowed to go alone on the streetcar.
And then I walked alone to school, which was also a miracle of miracles, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after I was 10.
Until that time, even-- it to the grade school, they walked me and picked me up.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So that was that.
I belonged to Girl Guides, and I liked it very much.
But they sent me one year also, just before I went to gymnasium, to a Girl Guide camp, and I hated it from the bottom of my heart.
Really hated it.
Slept in tents.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I didn't hate it because of the tents.
I hated it because I had no friends there.
It was a completely new group, you know?
And girls are very cliquey.
So it was not a success.
How did your family spend holiday time?
Well, first of all, my father-- if you notice that my father never spent any of his leisure time with us, and never on any of his holidays with us.
So-- and so it was always my mother and I.
And sometimes we would go to the mountains in Czechoslovakia.
And sometimes she would take me to Austria and to Italy.
It was very nice, very, very nice.
Once my father met us in Austria, in Salzburg, for-- for a week.
But-- and they were long holidays.
They were nice holidays.
And then-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but I was little, you know.
Then, after '38, after Munich, there were really no-- we went once or twice to some spots around Prague, or to little places where you could swim, but not for long.
Then it was very, very tense already.
Oh, my father was fun.
He-- I could never do any wrong with my father.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father was a yeller.
He yelled, but he didn't mean it.
Not at me.
Marek.
And the family called him-- the Czech family called him Mirko, M-I-R-K-O.
And only later, Marek.
And--
Vonchovska.
And he-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he had breakfast very early in the morning.
At 6:30 I heard him in the dining room, and sometimes I would get up and keep him company.
He always had a glass of tea and a piece of coffee cake.
And when he came home for lunch, religiously, at 1:00, sometimes I was home and sometimes I wasn't home.
And he-- he would lie down for half an hour after lunch.
And the maid was not around-- allowed to wash dishes, because it disturbed him.
And then he left.
And then he didn't come home till 8:30, and he had supper alone.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had our big meal at-- at lunch.
And sometimes I didn't-- I wasn't there.
So usually, if so, we ate together on Sundays.
In '94, I believe. 1894.
Yes, March 12.
In Warsaw.
Yes.
I know about my father's family a little bit.
I mean, I know that he adored his mother.
His father was a very cruel man who never looked after his family properly.
He constantly went to the study groups and studied the Talmud and interpretation.
And his mother ran a little store with his sisters.
He had a brother who lived in Danzig, and he was a watch maker.
I mean-- and he visited us once in Prague.
His name was Felix.
I don't even remember how he looked, but I know he was there.
And my father had a sister whose name was Regina.
And she also visited us once in Prague from Poland.
And she brought me-- she brought my mother a leather purse, which was grey, and she brought me a red leather purse, which was small and it was like a chess board, red and black, small.
That I remember.
I-- I couldn't have been more than five.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was all I know about them.
I know that my grandfather, whom I never meant, he was very, very cruel to my father.
And he ran away from home.
And then [INAUDIBLE] when my father was a rich man, he sent them money all the time, every month.
And he visited them in Warsaw, twice.
I know, because the family talked about it, my mother's family.
And he stayed in the best hotel in Warsaw and-- and was saying ha ha to them, I guess.
I don't know.
You know, [INAUDIBLE] to his father.
But he always supported them.
He came to Czechoslovakia, I think it was 1910 or 1912, I couldn't tell you.
But he ran away from home.
He had a tra-- a trade.
He was a silversmith.
I mean, one son was a watchmaker, one son was a silversmith.
And he was already working and earning money.
And he had to give all the money to his father, who gave some of it to his mother.
And-- but whatever he gave to his mother, she kept some of it for him, because she knew his plan.
He decided he will not last at home and he ran away, because once his father was mad at him and he didn't let him come into the house in the middle of winter, and he got pneumonia and almost died.
And he decided he cannot stand this treatment, and there must be other ways to live.
And he decided he'll go to Vienna, because that was the center of this silver and gold business.
And when he got-- he really ran away, his mother knew about it.
He bought himself some pants, he always told us, and-- and a warm jacket.
And he bought a ticket to Krakow, where he was supposed to change trains for Vienna.
And he missed the train for Vienna, and there was a train going to Prague.
So he decided one must be as good as the other, and he went to Prague.
And in Prague he-- it wasn't difficult for him to get a job, because people who very lived-- who knew how to do it, how to work, there were not many.
So he got a very good job, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] started saving.
He lived-- he rented a room somewhere.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I think he met somebody at the train station, a lady who spoke Yiddish or who looked Jewish.
And she gave him a name, and they rented him a room with-- with-- with board.
He also made a vow never to go to a synagogue and never to eat kosher again.
Those were his decision for life, and he kept them.
And he started to save.
And then came the war, you know.
And he-- he was stateless, and he-- he was not conscripted.
For World War I, because he was considered for Russian and-- he was basically stateless.
He had no papers.
And the-- the factory where he was employed, they were going full blast also with-- probably with some war industry, because the machinery could be very easily converted.
So he made a lot of money during the war.
And in 1921, he put a-- an ad in a Czech paper and in the German paper, in which means the Prague Daily Paper, which was like the-- the New York Times for-- in German, for Prague.
And then there was [INAUDIBLE], the Czech Word.
That he was looking for apartment to open a business.
And my mother's family were looking for their youngest son to do something, because he was a little bit hard of hearing, my uncle.
And maybe that's why my father yelled so much.
And so my mother had another brother.
She had four brothers and one sister.
And the other brother was a lawyer.
And my grandfather decided he will not invest money unless the-- his son, the lawyer, looks this guy over.
After all, he came-- it's a Polish Jew, you don't trust.
You know, the Czechs don't trust.
So they-- he said when my father described, you could die laughing.
They really interviewed him.
It was like a confession, it wasn't an interview.
So finally they said yes, and they rented little rooms in a basement.
And they did extremely well, extremely well.
From day one on, they became very, very rich.
It didn't-- the depression didn't touch them.
Nothing touched them.
They-- they really did extremely well.
And then--
They used their own name, Vonchovska and Stein, silver and gold were manufacturing.

And they-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and then the refugees from Germany and Austria started to come in from the Sudeten, after all, after Munich, so there were friends who left, who applied, who left everything, who applied for passports and get themselves and their families out.
And my mother's family said nein, that cannot happen in Prague.
That cannot happen in Czechoslovakia.
That's impossible.
So they stayed.
My mother was a Czech patriot.
She came from a small town south of Prague.
And as I said, my grandfather had a-- a wholesale business with the grain.
Joseph Stein.
He was selling grain.
And it was an interesting family, two sisters married to brothers.
The other brother was manufacturing liquor, and he was selling grain.
And then during-- then they retired to Prague.
When he retired, he retired to Prague because all his children were in Prague.
That's my grandfather, I mean.
I-- I didn't know them, really.
I don't-- they were around for a year or two, but I was two, three years old.
I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe my grandmother, I vaguely remember, but--
Yes, Rosa.
Rosalie.
Mm-hm.
Kamila, Kamila.
And--
K-A-M-I-L-A.
Steinova.
You know, in Czech you always put this -ova at the end, or a.
S-T-E-I-N-O-V-A.
'96 in November, 8th of November.
Yes.
In Tabor, in this town, T-A-B-O-R.
Like Mount Tabor.
Yes.
I had no siblings.
I was an only child.
And my mother's oldest brother was politically very engaged, because when you asked me the last time-- and he was a Social Democrat.
And he paid for his life for it.
He-- he was arrested already in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in 1939, in October.
And he was shipped-- they-- they were crazy.
They were demonstrating in front of the Prague Gestapo with the Czech flags.
And-- and they shipped him to Saxonhausen to the main camp.
And in February, the family got an urn at that point, still, with his ashes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But he was-- he was really a Czech with a capital C.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The others, they just drifted away.
Nobody believed what would happen.
No.
I'm-- I'm-- I'm telling you, my-- my friend was my cousin, this Jirka.
He was older than I, but we-- when we saw each other, when our mothers got together, so we saw each other, so we were great friends.
My three cousins, daughters of this crazy uncle, political, they were much older than I.
They were about-- the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the oldest one was as old as my mother, or older.
And the youngest one is-- is the wife of the gentleman who died in London.
And she is then years older than I am.
And we are like sisters.
She was during the war in London.
Well, he didn't really reject Judaism.
He rejected any religion in what-- whatever form, and-- and with it, all the trappings, you know, all the traditions, everything.
Just-- as I told you, just a little bit-- a little bit about Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur, that was the beginning and the end of it.
I suppose you had a festive dinner the night before.
And all I-- all my aunts and uncles came for coffee on first day of Rosh Hashanah to our house.
And Yom Kippur, my mother celebrated.
The-- the night before we had chicken soup, chicken dinner with noodles.
And the next day she fasted half a day.
My father didn't.
He didn't do anything.
Well, my mother and her sister, for Yom Kippur.
And maybe my-- whether my uncle, I-- I have no idea.
Yes, this-- it's the one in Prague, [INAUDIBLE] but it didn't really have a name.
It-- it was named after the street it was on.
Yes, of course.
It's a capital S-A-Z-A-V-S-K-A.
That's how it was known.
The temple in Sazavska, they called it.
It-- it had no-- no specific name.
Oh yes, when-- when my mother went, so maybe I went with my-- one of my uncles, I don't remember.
But I was always downstairs.
And on Yom Kippur, I remember I brought my mother some flowers.
She was sitting in the gallery.
Not much.
It didn't leave a great impression on me.
No, it-- it doesn't stand anymore.
But it-- it's strange, because the synagogues in Prague, even if they were destroyed-- not destroyed.
They were not destroyed.
They were used as warehouses for furniture that the Germans moved out of Jewish flats.
Czech.
Absolutely Czech.
It was-- [INAUDIBLE] it-- it didn't matter too much.
No.
It was March.
It was March 15, 1939.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] It started already with Munich, which was in September, '38.
And then the situation was very grave.
And the-- the Czech ar-- the Czechoslovakian army was mobilize.
And we were issued gas masks.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- and then nothing happened.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then it went from-- then who had any sense, any rationality in their-- [INAUDIBLE] left, left.
Because you-- you heard it on the radio, there was just no way.
People were streaming into Prague, from Sudetenland, from Germany, from Austria, because they-- Austria was already annexed in-- in February or March, '38.
So these people were not telling lies.
They-- it to happened to them.
And they-- they ran to Czechoslovakia because it was open, and they tried to get visas for England, America, Canada, at that point, even France, you know, who would have them.
And yet the Prague Jews sat happily on their behinds.
And very few did anything.
Very few.
They-- they gave them shelter, and they were sorry for everybody.
And they-- there were communal kitchens and everything, but they, themselves, didn't move.
They didn't think it would touch them, which was, of course, ridiculous.
When-- [INAUDIBLE] I was 12.
And then when Hitler marched into Prague, I was 12 years old.
It was a very-- somebody phoned us at 4:00 in the morning that Hitler marched in from-- they marched in from the-- from all sides, basically from the east, northeast.
And it was a snowy morning, Ides of March.
Absolute.
Absolute.
I knew it was the end.
You grow up very fast.
You stop being a child.
My-- the governess left.
She left after Munich.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so it was-- and then a terrible thing happened to me that was a few months later.
I came home from school, on-- I was already in that language school, not in the gymnasium anymore.
And on December 3, 1939, I came home from school.
It was a Saturday.
And I found the housekeeper, Mania, crying hysterically.
And my two uncles were there, and the-- the apartment was in utter shambles.
And my parents were arrested.
And that was that.
They were looking for-- it was a-- one of the German workers in the factory who wanted to take over the factory-- which he did, eventually, for a year, and then the-- then somebody bought it.
So he-- he denounced my father, my mother, and my uncle at something that is called-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not the Gestapo.
It was called the Wiesenschutz commander.
They were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- the reason is for an exchange.
And it was a-- an office to protect the foreign exchange, meaning the hard currency and gold.
And-- and this guy denounced-- I know also his name-- denounced my parents and my uncle that they were-- they had gold at home.
And the Gestapo came while I was at school, and they really ransacked the apartment.
They--
OK.
